name: Jasper AI

pre_launch.description: AI copywriting tool built on GPT-3, launched as Jarvis then rebranded to Jasper

month_1.description: Rapid growth through affiliate marketing and word-of-mouth

year_1.description: Reached $80M+ ARR, became category leader in AI writing

failure_reasons: ChatGPT commoditized core offering overnight
Dependent on OpenAI API (no own model)
Enterprise pivot came too late
CEO departure created leadership vacuum
Revenue declined significantly as free alternatives emerged

key_events.event: ChatGPT launched, directly competing with core product
CEO Dave Rogenmoser stepped down, Timothy Young took over
Pivoted to enterprise B2B marketing solutions

notes: Classic case of a GPT-wrapper being disrupted by the underlying model provider launching a direct competitor. Peak valuation $1.5B.

name: Stability AI

pre_launch.description: AI research lab focused on open-source generative AI

month_1.description: Released Stable Diffusion, became viral open-source phenomenon

year_1.description: Became most recognized open-source AI image generator

success_reasons: Stable Diffusion became industry-standard open-source model
failure_reasons: Burning $100M+ annually with insufficient revenue
CEO Emad Mostaque resigned amid crisis
Multiple rounds of layoffs (30%+ staff)
Failed to monetize open-source model effectively
Intense competition from Midjourney, DALL-E 3, Flux

key_events.event: Pivoted toward commercial products and partnerships

notes: Opened the open-source AI image generation space but couldn't monetize. Valuation dropped from $1B+ to distressed levels.

name: Inflection AI (Pi)

pre_launch.description: Founded by Mustafa Suleyman (ex-DeepMind) and Karen Simonyan
pre_launch.key_metric: raised $225M+ from Microsoft, Reid Hoffman, others

month_1.description: Launched Pi personal intelligence chatbot

year_1.description: Built emotional AI persona but struggled to find sustainable business model

success_reasons: Strong founding team with DeepMind pedigree
failure_reasons: No clear revenue model
Consumer AI chatbot space extremely crowded
Microsoft absorbed talent and technology
Effectively reverse acqui-hired by Microsoft
Product discontinued after team departure

key_events.event: Raised $225M+ from Microsoft, Reid Hoffman, Greylock
Microsoft hired most of Inflection staff (~70 employees)
Mustafa Suleyman became CEO of Microsoft AI division
Pi shut down, infrastructure transitioned to Microsoft/AWS
Antitrust concerns raised about the arrangement

notes: Classic reverse acqui-hire. Microsoft got the team and tech without formal acquisition, raising antitrust questions. Product is dead.

name: Juicero

pre_launch.description: WiFi-connected cold-press juicer with proprietary produce packs
pre_launch.key_metric: raised $120M from Google Ventures, Kleiner Perkins

month_1.description: Launched at $399 + $5-8 per produce pack

year_1.description: Bloomberg revealed packs could be squeezed by hand, product became joke

success_reasons: Strong initial narrative about healthy living + tech
failure_reasons: Core product was over-engineered solution to simple problem
Bloomberg hand-squeeze video went viral, destroying value prop
$399 machine + expensive proprietary packs = absurd pricing
Extremely narrow target market
WiFi/barcode scanner unnecessary for juice packs
Became symbol of Silicon Valley excess

key_events.event: Company shut down, offered full refunds
CEO attempted to sell company, failed to find buyer

notes: Most famous example of over-engineering in Silicon Valley. $120M raised, destroyed by a 2-minute Bloomberg video.

name: Quibi

pre_launch.description: Short-form premium mobile streaming by Jeffrey Katzenberg & Meg Whitman
pre_launch.key_metric: raised $1.75B from Disney, NBCUniversal, Sony, AT&T

month_1.description: Launched April 2020 during COVID-19 pandemic
month_1.key_metric: ~500K subscribers at launch, far below targets

year_1.description: Shut down after ~6 months, sold content to Roku

failure_reasons: Launched during COVID when people were home watching TV, not phones
Paid content when TikTok/YouTube are free
No social sharing or screenshot capability
Content didn't resonate with audiences
Misread market demand for premium short content
Burned $1.75B in 6 months with minimal traction

key_events.event: Founded by Jeffrey Katzenberg and Meg Whitman
Officially shut down, ~6 months after launch

notes: $1.75B raised, dead in 6 months. Worst timing in startup history (pandemic + TikTok era).

name: Pebble

pre_launch.description: E-paper smartwatch with long battery life, launched on Kickstarter

month_1.description: Massive crowdfunding success, shipped 2M+ units total

year_1.description: Became category leader in smartwatch space

failure_reasons: Apple Watch launched April 2015, crushed competition
Samsung and Google also entered market
Struggled to raise additional VC funding
Revenue declined as Apple dominated
Couldn't compete with Apple's ecosystem and brand
Hardware margins too thin

key_events.event: Apple Watch launched, beginning of decline
Fitbit acquired assets for ~$23M (vs $740M peak valuation)

notes: Pioneered the smartwatch but couldn't survive Apple Watch. Acquired for $23M vs $740M peak - a 97% value destruction.

name: Character.ai
target_user: entertainment users, roleplay enthusiasts, lonely individuals

pre_launch.description: AI chatbot platform for character-based conversations
pre_launch.key_metric: founded by ex-Google AI researchers Noam Shazeer and Daniel De Freitas

month_1.description: Rapid user growth, viral among younger demographics

year_1.description: Became top AI character platform, then lost key talent

success_reasons: Unique product-market fit for AI character conversations
Strong technical team with Google DeepMind pedigree
Novel use case beyond productivity AI
failure_reasons: No clear monetization path
High compute costs for conversational AI
Co-founders returned to Google (reverse acqui-hire)
~30 key researchers followed founders to Google
Google licensed technology for $2.7B, not a full acquisition
Pivoted away from core product toward education/productivity

key_events.event: Reached millions of users, peak engagement
Co-founders Noam Shazeer and Daniel De Freitas left for Google
~30 key researchers followed to Google DeepMind
Company pivoted to education and productivity tools

notes: Massive engagement but couldn't monetize. Lost founders and key talent to Google in a reverse acqui-hire deal worth $2.7B in licensing.

name: Meta Galactica

pre_launch.description: LLM trained on scientific papers for research assistance

month_1.description: Launched Nov 15, pulled Nov 17 (3 days)

year_1.description: Never relaunched publicly, research continued internally

success_reasons: Ambitious goal of AI for scientific research
Trained on massive corpus of academic papers
Interesting technical approach to citation-aware generation
failure_reasons: Generated fabricated scientific citations and papers
Misinformation concerns in scientific context
Pulled after just 3 days of public availability
No guardrails for hallucinated scientific content
Reputational damage to Meta AI

key_events.event: Meta pulled the demo after 3 days due to accuracy concerns

notes: Lived only 3 days as a public demo. Hallucinated scientific papers undermined trust. A cautionary tale about releasing AI in high-stakes domains.

name: Meta BlenderBot 3

pre_launch.description: Conversational AI research project by Meta AI

month_1.description: Released as public sandbox for research

year_1.description: Demo shut down May 2024, research continued internally

success_reasons: Open to public for feedback and research
failure_reasons: Produced offensive and inaccurate responses
Public demo generated bad PR
No clear product or business application
Meta shifted focus to Llama and Meta AI assistant
Research demo never became a product
notes: Research demo that never became a product. Shut down after Meta pivoted strategy to Llama-based products.

name: Amazon CodeWhisperer

pre_launch.description: AWS AI-powered code generation tool

month_1.description: Competed with GitHub Copilot in AWS ecosystem

year_1.description: Replaced by Amazon Q Developer in 2024

failure_reasons: Couldn't compete with GitHub Copilot's dominance
AWS folded it into broader Amazon Q product
Standalone identity lost in rebrand
Developer adoption remained limited vs Copilot

key_events.event: CodeWhisperer officially sunset, users migrated to Amazon Q Developer

notes: Not a failure per se, but a product that was absorbed and discontinued. AWS decided standalone coding assistant couldn't compete, folded into Amazon Q.

name: Jawbone

pre_launch.description: Bluetooth headsets pivoted to wearable fitness trackers (UP series)

month_1.description: UP tracker launched, gained early adopter traction

year_1.description: Peak popularity, then quality issues and competition emerged

failure_reasons: Product quality issues (tracker reliability problems)
Fitbit and Apple Watch dominated the market
Inventory and supply chain challenges
High burn rate despite massive funding
Failed pivot to enterprise/clinical health monitoring
Filed for liquidation

key_events.event: Pivoted from Bluetooth headsets to UP fitness trackers
Quality issues led to recalls and returns

notes: Raised $930M+ and still failed. Quality issues + competition from Fitbit and Apple Watch. One of the biggest hardware failures in tech.

name: WeWork

pre_launch.description: Co-working space startup by Adam Neumann, backed by SoftBank

month_1.description: Rapid expansion, premium co-working brand

year_1.description: Peak $47B valuation, then spectacular collapse

failure_reasons: Unsustainable lease model (long-term leases, short-term subleases)
Massive overexpansion
Corporate governance failures
Adam Neumann's erratic management
IPO withdrawal in 2019 after S-1 revealed massive losses
COVID-19 accelerated remote work, reducing office demand
$19B in long-term lease obligations

key_events.event: Peak $47B valuation ahead of planned IPO
IPO withdrawn after S-1 filing revealed massive losses
Adam Neumann forced out as CEO
Filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy with $19B lease obligations

notes: $47B to bankruptcy. The defining story of overfunded startup excess. SoftBank lost billions.

name: Zenefits

pre_launch.description: HR SaaS platform for benefits, payroll, onboarding

month_1.description: Rapid growth, disrupted insurance brokerage

year_1.description: Reached $4.5B valuation, then regulatory scandal hit

failure_reasons: Regulatory scandal: software auto-filled fake insurance license numbers
CEO Parker Conrad resigned under pressure
Multiple rounds of massive layoffs
Valuation written down from $4.5B to ~$2B then lower
Forced pivot from SaaS to brokerage model
Acquired by Rippling for ~$350M (92% discount from peak)

key_events.event: CEO Parker Conrad resigned over licensing scandal
David Sacks took over, then Jay Fulcher became CEO

notes: $4.5B to $350M acquisition (92% value destruction). Regulatory scandal destroyed trust. Classic case of growth-at-all-costs backfiring.

name: Google Stadia

pre_launch.description: Google's cloud gaming platform promising console-quality gaming anywhere

month_1.description: Launched with limited game library and required hardware purchases

year_1.description: First-party studio shut down, declining interest

success_reasons: Promised AAA gaming without expensive hardware
failure_reasons: Limited game library, few exclusives
Google's reputation for killing products undermined trust
Weak marketing and consumer awareness
Competition from Xbox Cloud Gaming, GeForce Now
Required upfront hardware purchase
First-party studio shut down before making any games
Developer ecosystem never materialized

key_events.event: Shut down Stadia Games & Entertainment (first-party studio)
Stadia officially shut down, all hardware/game purchases refunded

notes: A entry in the 'Google Graveyard'. Technically impressive but commercially dead. Google refunded everything, showing it knew it failed.

name: Clubhouse

pre_launch.description: Invite-only audio social networking app

month_1.description: Viral growth, celebrity users, $4B valuation

year_1.description: Copycats emerged, downloads plummeted
year_1.key_metric: downloads dropped from millions to near-bottom of app stores

failure_reasons: Twitter Spaces, Spotify Greenroom, Discord Stage copied the format
Platform incumbents had distribution advantages
Failed to retain casual users after hype faded
No monetization for creators (unlike YouTube/TikTok)
Pivoted to group messaging, losing core identity
Laid off 50%+ staff in 2023
Effectively shut down as standalone app by 2024

key_events.event: Elon Musk interview went viral, downloads spiked
Twitter Spaces launched, first major copycat
Downloads plummeted as novelty wore off
Effectively shut down as standalone app, pivoted to messaging

notes: $4B valuation to effectively dead in ~2 years. The defining story of hype-driven valuations without sustainable product-market fit. Copykilled by platform incumbents.

name: Humane AI Pin

pre_launch.description: Laser-projected display wearable AI device by ex-Apple executives

month_1.description: Launched at $699 + $24/month, terrible reviews

year_1.description: Company exploring sale of assets

success_reasons: Strong founding team (ex-Apple executives Imran Chaudhri, Bethany Bongiorno)
failure_reasons: Slow, unreliable AI responses
Overheating and short battery life
Impractical laser projector in real-world use
$699 + $24/month too expensive for unproven device
Solved problems that didn't exist
Only ~10,000 units sold
High return rates
Became symbol of AI hardware overhype

key_events.event: Announced at TED talk with major hype
Terrible reviews from MKBHD, The Verge, Linus Tech Tips
Only ~10,000 units sold, high return rates
Company exploring sale of company or assets

notes: Raised $230M+, sold ~10K units at $699. The poster child for AI hardware hype overdelivering on promises and underdelivering on reality.

name: Rabbit R1

pre_launch.description: $199 AI device with Large Action Model (LAM) for app control
pre_launch.key_metric: unveiled at CES 2024 with major hype

month_1.description: Launched to disappointing reviews

year_1.description: Became cautionary tale of AI hardware hype

success_reasons: Novel LAM concept for AI-driven app control
Rabbit Inc had previous product (rabbit r1 app)
failure_reasons: Most features already done better on smartphones
Slow, laggy, unreliable performance
LAM promise fell short in real-world tests
Poor battery life and build quality
'Solution looking for a problem'
Negative reviews from MKBHD, The Verge, Linus Tech Tips
Companion to Humane AI Pin as AI hardware cautionary tale

key_events.event: Unveiled at CES 2024 with major hype
Terrible reviews from major tech reviewers
Became symbol of AI hardware overpromise

notes: CES hype to disappointing reality. Alongside Humane AI Pin, represents the 2024 AI hardware hype cycle's failure to deliver.

name: Arc Browser
target_user: power users, tech enthusiasts, productivity seekers

pre_launch.description: Radically redesigned browser by The Browser Company

month_1.description: Critical acclaim, passionate niche community
month_1.key_metric: strong positive reviews, growing user base

year_1.description: Praised but couldn't achieve mass adoption, pivoted to Dia

success_reasons: Radical UX innovation (Spaces, Command Bar, Profiles)
failure_reasons: Too complex for average users
Sub-1% market share vs Chrome's ~65%
Monetization strategy unclear
Pivoted away from Arc to new browser 'Dia'
Community felt abandoned by the pivot
Couldn't compete with Chrome's ecosystem and defaults
VC pressure for growth in a winner-take-all market

key_events.event: CEO Josh Miller announced pivot to new browser 'Dia'
Arc development scaled back, community backlash

notes: Beloved by power users but couldn't scale. The pivot to Dia acknowledged that Arc alone wouldn't win the browser war. Sub-1% market share.

name: Vine
target_user: content creators, Gen Z, social media users

pre_launch.description: 6-second looping video app, acquired by Twitter pre-launch

month_1.description: Massive viral growth, created internet celebrities
month_1.key_metric: millions of daily active users at peak

year_1.description: Became cultural phenomenon, then began decline

success_reasons: Created new category of content creators
failure_reasons: Twitter never monetized it properly
No creator monetization (unlike YouTube)
Instagram Video and Snapchat Stories copied the format
Top creators left for YouTube/Instagram where they could earn money
Twitter failed to invest in product development
No desktop version launched
TikTok later dominated the format Vine invented

key_events.event: Vine founded by Dom Hofmann, Rus Yusupov, Colin Kroll
Twitter acquired Vine before public launch
Peak cultural relevance, top creators emerging

notes: Invented the short-form video format that TikTok later dominated. Twitter's failure to monetize and invest killed what could have been TikTok.

name: Mozilla Boot to Gecko (B2G/Firefox OS)

pre_launch.description: HTML5-based mobile OS for low-cost smartphones

month_1.description: Launched on budget phones in emerging markets

year_1.description: Struggled against Android's dominance, discontinued

failure_reasons: Android dominated emerging markets with Google services
No app ecosystem compared to Android/iOS
Carrier and OEM support faded
Mozilla缺乏 resources to compete with Google
Developers didn't build for the platform
Firefox OS discontinued in 2016
B2G repos abandoned by 2021

key_events.event: Shipped on budget phones in developing countries
Firefox OS officially discontinued for smartphones
Last commits on B2G GitHub repositories

notes: Open-source mobile OS for budget smartphones. Last commits 2021. Android dominated emerging markets, killing Firefox OS.
